questions.prompt: In the automation pyramid, which layer should have the most tests?
questions.explanation: Unit tests form the wide base. They are fast and cheap and pinpoint failures to one function, so you write many of them and run them constantly.

questions.prompt: Why are end-to-end tests kept few and near the top of the pyramid?
options: They are too easy to write
They run the fastest of all the layers
They are slow, prone to flakiness, and expensive to maintain because they touch the whole stack
They can only test one function at a time
questions.explanation: E2E tests drive the real app through the whole stack, so they are slow, brittle, and costly to maintain. They are reserved for critical user journeys, not logic a unit test could check.

options: Writing tests only at the integration layer
An inverted pyramid: many slow, brittle end-to-end tests and almost no unit tests
Running all tests on a single machine
Skipping automation entirely in favour of manual testing
questions.explanation: The ice-cream cone flips the pyramid — a heavy top of UI tests balanced on a thin base of unit tests. The result is a slow, flaky suite that is expensive to maintain and vague about where failures are.

questions.prompt: A piece of business logic could be verified by either a unit test or an end-to-end test. Which is the better default choice and why?
options: The end-to-end test, because it is closer to the user
The unit test, because it runs far faster and isolates the failure to one place
Neither — logic should only be tested manually
Whichever one is quicker to write, regardless of run cost
questions.explanation: Push logic checks down to the cheapest layer that can verify them. A unit test runs in milliseconds and points straight at the failing function, while an e2e test is far slower and flakier for the same coverage.
